# Kubernetes API Server: Subsystem Architecture and Extension Guide

## Table of Contents

1. [Subsystem Purpose and Boundaries](#1-subsystem-purpose-and-boundaries)
2. [Key Components and Responsibility Split](#2-key-components-and-responsibility-split)
3. [End-to-End Request Flow](#3-end-to-end-request-flow)
4. [The Watch Cache and Storage Layer](#4-the-watch-cache-and-storage-layer)
5. [Extension Points and Customization Hooks](#5-extension-points-and-customization-hooks)
6. [Failure Modes, Limits, and Design Tradeoffs](#6-failure-modes-limits-and-design-tradeoffs)
7. [Operational Implications](#7-operational-implications)
8. [File and Directory Map](#8-file-and-directory-map)

---

## 1. Subsystem Purpose and Boundaries

The `k8s.io/apiserver` library (`staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/`) is the shared framework from which every Kubernetes API server is built. It is not a standalone binary; it is a Go library providing the scaffolding that the three concrete servers composing `kube-apiserver`, as well as any extension API server built with the aggregation pattern, embed directly.

Its responsibilities span the full lifetime of an API request:
- Accepting and terminating TLS connections.
- Authenticating callers and authorizing their intent.
- Throttling and prioritizing traffic with API Priority and Fairness.
- Dispatching requests to per-resource REST handlers.
- Coordinating admission control (mutation and validation).
- Persisting state to and watching from etcd, with an in-process watch cache as an intermediary.
- Emitting audit events and serving observability endpoints.

**Upstream dependencies** the library consumes:
- `k8s.io/apimachinery` — the `runtime.Scheme`, `GroupVersionKind`, serialization codecs, and object lifecycle types.
- `k8s.io/client-go` — informers and REST clients (primarily used within the admission subsystem and post-start hooks).
- `go.etcd.io/etcd/client/v3` — the etcd client, accessed only through the `pkg/storage/etcd3` package.
- `k8s.io/kube-openapi` — the OpenAPI v2/v3 spec builder.

**Downstream consumers**:
- `cmd/kube-apiserver` — the main Kubernetes control plane API server.
- `staging/src/k8s.io/kube-aggregator` — the aggregation layer that delegates to extension API servers.
- `staging/src/k8s.io/apiextensions-apiserver` — the CRD handler.
- Any extension API server built with `RecommendedOptions` (the `sample-apiserver` pattern).

The library deliberately has no knowledge of any specific resource type (Pod, Deployment, etc.). Those are registered at startup by the consuming binary through the `APIGroupInfo` and `runtime.Scheme` mechanisms.

---

## 2. Key Components and Responsibility Split

### 2.1 Configuration and Bootstrap (`pkg/server/`)

Configuration flows through a layered struct hierarchy before any server starts:

**`RecommendedOptions`** (`pkg/server/options/recommended.go`): A convenience aggregate of all the option structs an extension API server typically needs — etcd, TLS serving, authentication, authorization, audit, admission, egress, and tracing. Each field maps 1-to-1 to a domain-specific options struct (e.g., `EtcdOptions`, `AuditOptions`). When `ApplyTo` is called, each option struct populates the corresponding section of the `Config`.

**`Config`** (`pkg/server/config.go:112`): The central struct that captures all runtime configuration for a `GenericAPIServer`. Notable fields:
- `Authentication AuthenticationInfo` and `Authorization AuthorizationInfo` — pluggable auth.
- `AdmissionControl admission.Interface` — the assembled admission chain.
- `FlowControl utilflowcontrol.Interface` — API Priority and Fairness; if nil, falls back to the simpler `MaxRequestsInFlight` semaphore.
- `BuildHandlerChainFunc` — a replaceable hook that constructs the HTTP middleware stack. Defaults to `DefaultBuildHandlerChain`.
- `RESTOptionsGetter genericregistry.RESTOptionsGetter` — factory for per-resource storage backend configuration.
- `ResourceTransformers storagevalue.ResourceTransformers` — the encryption-at-rest transformer chain.

**`GenericAPIServer`** (`pkg/server/genericapiserver.go:109`): The server runtime object. It holds the HTTP handler (`Handler *APIServerHandler`), the delegation target for the next server in the chain (`delegationTarget DelegationTarget`), lifecycle signals, health check registries, and the audit backend. After all API groups are installed, `PrepareRun()` finalizes health endpoints and OpenAPI spec installation, and `RunWithContext()` starts the TLS listener.

### 2.2 Server Delegation Chain

The `kube-apiserver` binary is not a single server but a **chain of three `GenericAPIServer` instances** constructed in `cmd/kube-apiserver/app/server.go:CreateServerChain` (line 176):

```
CreateServerChain():
  apiExtensionsServer = config.ApiExtensions.New(emptyDelegate)    // innermost
  kubeAPIServer       = config.KubeAPIs.New(apiExtensionsServer)
  aggregatorServer    = CreateAggregatorServer(kubeAPIServer, ...)  // outermost, receives all traffic
```

Each server implements `DelegationTarget` (`pkg/server/genericapiserver.go:308`). When a request arrives at the outermost server (the aggregator) and no `APIService` claims the path, the request is forwarded to the next delegate's `UnprotectedHandler()`. This continues until a server handles the request or the innermost `emptyDelegate` returns 404. The delegation is composable: post-start hooks, health checks, and lifecycle signals from all three servers are merged by the outermost server at `PrepareRun()` time.

### 2.3 HTTP Handler Chain (`pkg/server/filters/`, `pkg/endpoints/filters/`)

The HTTP middleware stack is assembled inside-out by `DefaultBuildHandlerChain` (`pkg/server/config.go:1015`). Because Go's net/http middleware wraps from outside in, the order of `handler = WithX(handler, ...)` calls is the reverse of request-arrival order. Reading the function bottom-to-top gives the actual order a request traverses:

1. `WithAuditInit` — allocates the audit event for the request.
2. `WithPanicRecovery` — catches panics and returns a 500.
3. `WithMuxAndDiscoveryComplete` — blocks requests with 503 until all routes are installed (prevents spurious 404s at startup).
4. `WithRequestReceivedTimestamp` — stamps the request start time.
5. `WithRequestInfo` — parses the URL into a `RequestInfo` struct (resource, namespace, name, verb) used by auth.
6. `WithRoutine` (optional, feature-gated) — dispatches downstream handlers to a goroutine to reduce stack depth.
7. `WithLatencyTrackers`, `WithHTTPLogging`, `WithHSTS`, `WithCacheControl` — observability and HTTP hygiene.
8. `WithRetryAfter` (optional) — during shutdown, returns 429 with `Retry-After` instead of accepting new work.
9. `WithWaitGroup` / `WithWatchTerminationDuringShutdown` — drain tracking for graceful shutdown.
10. `WithRequestDeadline` — enforces overall request timeout from config.
11. `WithTimeoutForNonLongRunningRequests` — runs the downstream handler in a goroutine with a deadline.
12. `WithWarningRecorder` — collects deprecation warnings to emit as response headers.
13. `WithCORS` — sets CORS headers.
14. `WithAuthentication` — calls the union authenticator; on failure routes to `failedHandler`.
15. `WithTracing` (optional, feature-gated) — starts an OpenTelemetry span after authentication so the caller's identity is available.
16. `WithAudit` — logs the audit event at the `RequestReceived` stage.
17. `WithImpersonation` / `WithConstrainedImpersonation` — allows callers with permission to act as another identity.
18. `WithPriorityAndFairness` (or `WithMaxInFlightLimit`) — classifies and queues the request.
19. `WithAuthorization` — checks if the authenticated identity is permitted to perform the requested verb on the resource.

The final destination after this chain is the `APIServerHandler`, which routes to go-restful (for API resources) or the non-restful mux (for `/metrics`, `/healthz`, `/openapi`, etc.).

### 2.4 API Group Registration and `APIInstaller` (`pkg/endpoints/`)

Serving a resource type requires two steps: defining storage and registering it.

**`APIGroupInfo`** (`pkg/server/genericapiserver.go:71`): A bundle that describes one API group:
- `PrioritizedVersions []schema.GroupVersion` — ordered list of versions; the first is the preferred version advertised to clients.
- `VersionedResourcesStorageMap map[string]map[string]rest.Storage` — maps `version → resource name → REST storage implementation`.
- `Scheme *runtime.Scheme` — the type registry and conversion hub for this group.
- `NegotiatedSerializer runtime.NegotiatedSerializer` — controls content-type negotiation (JSON, protobuf, CBOR).

`GenericAPIServer.InstallAPIGroups()` (line 876) calls `installAPIResources`, which creates an `APIGroupVersion` per version, then calls `APIInstaller.Install()` (`pkg/endpoints/installer.go`). The installer iterates the `VersionedResourcesStorageMap` and, for each resource, performs interface-based capability detection on the `rest.Storage` implementation. If the storage also implements `rest.Getter`, a GET handler is registered. If it implements `rest.Creater`, a POST handler is registered, and so on. This pattern means that the set of HTTP verbs exposed for a resource is determined entirely by which `rest.*` interfaces its storage object implements.

### 2.5 `genericregistry.Store` (`pkg/registry/generic/registry/store.go`)

`Store` is the canonical implementation of `rest.StandardStorage`. Extension API servers and all built-in Kubernetes resources embed it. It holds:

- `CreateStrategy rest.RESTCreateStrategy`, `UpdateStrategy rest.RESTUpdateStrategy`, `DeleteStrategy rest.RESTDeleteStrategy` — the **Strategy** pattern: resource-specific business logic (validation, defaulting, name generation, garbage collection handling) separated from generic CRUD mechanics.
- `BeginCreate BeginCreateFunc`, `BeginUpdate BeginUpdateFunc` — optional hooks that return a commit/rollback function, executed around the storage write (line 66–75). Almost never needed; the comment in the source recommends against their use.
- `AfterCreate AfterCreateFunc`, `AfterUpdate AfterUpdateFunc`, `AfterDelete AfterDeleteFunc` — post-write hooks that run before the decorator.
- `Decorator func(runtime.Object)` — applies non-stored transformations to objects returned from the store (line 170). Not watched.
- `Storage DryRunnableStorage` — wraps the underlying `storage.Interface`, intercepting dry-run requests.
- `PredicateFunc` — resource-specific label/field filter, used to push down list/watch predicates toward storage.

### 2.6 Storage Layer (`pkg/storage/`)

**`storage.Interface`** (`pkg/storage/interfaces.go:169`): The abstract storage contract. Operations: `Create`, `Delete`, `Watch`, `Get`, `GetList`, `GuaranteedUpdate`. The `GuaranteedUpdate` method accepts an `UpdateFunc` that is retried on conflict, implementing the optimistic-concurrency write loop inside the storage layer.

**`etcd3.store`** (`pkg/storage/etcd3/store.go`): The only production implementation of `storage.Interface`. It speaks to etcd via the `clientv3` library and the Kubernetes-specific `go.etcd.io/etcd/client/v3/kubernetes` variant. Each object is serialized (and optionally encrypted via `value.Transformer`) before being written. The `resourceVersion` of an object is the etcd `mod_revision` of the key cast to a string — this is why `resourceVersion` is globally ordered across all resource types.

**`Cacher`** (`pkg/storage/cacher/cacher.go`): A `storage.Interface` wrapper that interposes an in-memory reflector between the etcd client and callers. Initialized with a `storage.Interface` and a `Config` (line 80), it performs a LIST to snapshot state, then opens a persistent WATCH from that `resourceVersion`. Most `Get`, `GetList`, and `Watch` calls are served from the in-memory store. Bookmark events (sent at `defaultBookmarkFrequency = 1 minute`) advance the cacher's known `resourceVersion` even when objects are idle, preventing expensive resyncs.

### 2.7 Admission Control (`pkg/admission/`)

The admission system is a chain of plugins ordered by the `Plugins` registry. The chain type (`chainAdmissionHandler`, `pkg/admission/chain.go:23`) implements two separate passes:

- **`Admit`** (mutation) — calls every plugin that implements `MutationInterface` and handles the request operation. First error aborts the chain.
- **`Validate`** (validation) — calls every plugin that implements `ValidationInterface`. First error aborts.

Both passes share the `Attributes` interface (`pkg/admission/interfaces.go:31`), which exposes the deserialized object (`GetObject()`), the old object (`GetOldObject()` for updates), the user, the GVK, the operation, and a dry-run flag. Importantly, the object is deserialized and converted to the internal hub version before entering admission, so plugins always see the internal representation regardless of which wire version the client sent.

Webhook-based plugins (`MutatingWebhookConfiguration`, `ValidatingWebhookConfiguration`) and CEL-based policy plugins (`ValidatingAdmissionPolicy`, `MutatingAdmissionPolicy`) are layered on top of this same `MutationInterface`/`ValidationInterface` contract via the `pkg/admission/plugin/policy/generic/Plugin` struct.

### 2.8 Audit (`pkg/audit/`)

The audit subsystem is policy-driven. An `AuditPolicyRuleEvaluator` (configured via `pkg/audit/policy/checker.go`) matches each request against an ordered rule list, producing an audit `Level` (None, Metadata, Request, RequestResponse) and the stages to log (`RequestReceived`, `ResponseStarted`, `ResponseComplete`, `Panic`). The `WithAudit` filter in the handler chain populates an `auditinternal.Event` object stored in the request context; individual stages are written to the `AuditBackend` (log file or webhook) as they complete.

---

## 3. End-to-End Request Flow

The following traces a `kubectl apply` (a server-side apply PATCH) against a Deployment through the complete system.

### 3.1 Network Ingress to Handler Chain

The TLS listener (`pkg/server/secure_serving.go`) accepts the TCP connection and hands it to `net/http`'s `ServeTLS`. The Go HTTP/2 or HTTP/1.1 framer reads the request and calls the top of the handler chain assembled by `DefaultBuildHandlerChain`.

At this point the request body has not been read. The filters at the top of the chain (panic recovery, request info, timeout framing) operate on the URL and headers alone.

### 3.2 Authentication

`WithAuthentication` calls the **union authenticator**, which tries each registered authenticator in sequence — client-certificate (mTLS), bearer token (static or OIDC), service account JWT, request-header (for the aggregation proxy). The first to return a non-anonymous `user.Info` wins. If none succeed, `failedHandler` returns 401 and writes an audit event.

The resolved `user.Info` is stored in the request context via `request.WithUser`.

### 3.3 API Priority and Fairness

`WithPriorityAndFairness` (`pkg/server/filters/`) classifies the request by matching it against the cluster's `FlowSchema` objects. The matching produces a `PriorityLevel` and a `FlowDistinguisher` (e.g., user or namespace). The request is enqueued in the appropriate virtual queue. A work estimator (`flowcontrolrequest.NewWorkEstimator`) assigns a seat-count (width) and duration estimate to the request before queuing. If no seats are available, the request waits up to its deadline. If the queue is too long, the request is rejected with 429.

### 3.4 Authorization

`WithAuthorization` calls the **union authorizer** (RBAC, Node, Webhook, etc.). It passes a `DecisionRecord` containing the verb derived from the request method + URL and the resource parsed by `RequestInfoResolver`. The first authorizer to return `Allow` or `Deny` wins; `NoOpinion` passes to the next. A denial produces a 403.

### 3.5 Routing and Deserialization

The request reaches the go-restful `Container`, which matches the URL pattern (e.g., `/apis/apps/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/deployments/{name}`) to the handler registered by `APIInstaller`. For a PATCH request, this is the handler returned by `restfulPatchResource`.

Inside the handler (`pkg/endpoints/handlers/patch.go`), the request body is now read (`limitedReadBodyWithRecordMetric`) and deserialized. Content-type negotiation selects the appropriate decoder (JSON, protobuf, CBOR, or `application/apply-patch+yaml` for server-side apply). The object is decoded into the internal hub version via the `NegotiatedSerializer` and then converted using the `Scheme`.

### 3.6 Admission: Mutation Pass

The handler constructs an `admission.Attributes` record and calls `admit.Admit(ctx, admissionAttributes, scope)`. The chain iterates all `MutationInterface` plugins in order. For server-side apply, the `FieldManager` (`pkg/endpoints/handlers/fieldmanager/`) updates `managedFields` metadata to track field ownership. Webhook-based mutators may inject new fields; the reinvocation policy in `pkg/admission/reinvocation.go` may call mutation plugins a second time if any plugin indicated a change.

### 3.7 Admission: Validation Pass

After mutation is complete, `admit.Validate(ctx, admissionAttributes, scope)` is called. Each `ValidationInterface` plugin runs in sequence. CEL-based `ValidatingAdmissionPolicy` plugins compiled at policy-creation time evaluate their expressions against the (now-mutated) object. A validation failure returns a 422 Unprocessable Entity.

The resource's own `Strategy.Validate()` (inside `genericregistry.Store.Update`) provides a final, strategy-level validation pass that runs after admission.

### 3.8 Storage Write

`genericregistry.Store.Update` calls `Storage.GuaranteedUpdate`, which (via the `DryRunnableStorage` wrapper) delegates to `etcd3.store.GuaranteedUpdate`. The etcd store:

1. Reads the current value of the key with a `GET`.
2. Calls the `UpdateFunc` provided by the registry, which runs strategy-level precondition checks (`PrepareForUpdate`, `ValidateUpdate`).
3. Issues an etcd `txn` (compare-and-swap): if the current `mod_revision` matches the revision from step 1, write the new value. If not, retry from step 1.

The `resourceVersion` written into the returned object is the new `mod_revision` from etcd, converted to a string.

### 3.9 Watch Cache Propagation and Response

The etcd WATCH held by the `Cacher` sees the new revision event. It decodes the updated object, applies it to the in-memory store, and fans out the event to all registered `watchChan` subscribers (controllers, `kubectl --watch`, etc.).

Back in the request goroutine, the response is serialized to the negotiated content-type and written to the HTTP response. The audit `ResponseComplete` event is flushed to the audit backend.

---

## 4. The Watch Cache and Storage Layer

### 4.1 Cacher Initialization and Consistency

When the `Cacher` starts (`pkg/storage/cacher/cacher.go`), it performs a paginated `GetList` against etcd using `storageWatchListPageSize = 10000` objects per page (line 65). After the list completes at revision R, it opens a `WATCH` from R+1. All cache state is captured at revision R, and subsequent events flow in order from R+1 onwards.

For **consistent reads** (when a client passes a `resourceVersion` of `""` or requests an exact version), the cacher first fetches the latest etcd revision number without reading data (a cheap etcd linearizable read), then blocks until its internal `resourceVersion` catches up to that revision before serving from cache.

### 4.2 Watch Fanout

Each `Watch` call against the cacher returns a `watchChan` that receives a filtered stream of events. Events are dispatched to all registered watchers from a single goroutine. The `EventFreshDuration` (default ~75 seconds, line 72: `DefaultEventFreshDuration = defaultBookmarkFrequency + 15*time.Second`) defines the minimum event history window kept in memory. A client that reconnects within this window can resume without a full relist.

### 4.3 Encryption at Rest

Before bytes are written to etcd, they pass through the `value.Transformer` chain (`pkg/storage/value/`). The transformer is configured by the `EncryptionConfiguration` resource and can chain multiple providers: identity (no-op), secretbox (AES-256), KMS v1 (AES-CBC via gRPC), and KMS v2 (AES-GCM via gRPC, `pkg/storage/value/encrypt/envelope/kmsv2/`). Reads decode by trying each provider in order; writes use the first (current) provider. This allows live key rotation without downtime.

---

## 5. Extension Points and Customization Hooks

### 5.1 Building a New API Server with `RecommendedOptions`

The standard pattern for a new extension API server is to create a `RecommendedOptions` struct (`pkg/server/options/recommended.go:34`), call `ApplyTo` to populate a `server.RecommendedConfig`, then construct a `GenericAPIServer` and install custom `APIGroupInfo` structs. The `sample-apiserver` (`staging/src/k8s.io/sample-apiserver/`) demonstrates this pattern end-to-end.

Risk: Extension API servers share the same admission and auth configuration as the core server when accessed through the aggregation layer. If an extension server does not properly validate its own admission, the aggregation proxy will forward unauthenticated requests (it authenticates using the request-header credential).

### 5.2 Custom `Strategy` Objects

To add a built-in resource (not accessible without modifying the source), implement:
- `rest.RESTCreateStrategy` — `PrepareForCreate(ctx, obj)`, `Validate(ctx, obj) field.ErrorList`, `Canonicalize(obj)`.
- `rest.RESTUpdateStrategy` — `PrepareForUpdate(ctx, obj, old)`, `ValidateUpdate(ctx, obj, old) field.ErrorList`.
- `rest.RESTDeleteStrategy` — typically embedded from a default implementation.

Embed these in a `genericregistry.Store` and install via `InstallAPIGroups`. The strategy objects are the correct place for business logic that must be enforced even on in-cluster admin actions — unlike webhooks, they cannot be bypassed.

### 5.3 Custom Admission Plugins

Register a plugin with `admission.RegisterPlugin(name, factory)` in an `init()` function. The factory receives an `io.Reader` for per-plugin configuration. The plugin struct must implement at minimum `admission.Interface` (to report which operations it handles) and one of `MutationInterface` or `ValidationInterface`.

To access cluster state (e.g., to look up a Namespace during admission), implement the `initializer.WantsInformerFactory` or `initializer.WantsExternalKubeClientSet` interfaces. The `admission.PluginInitializer` mechanism (`pkg/admission/initializer/`) injects these dependencies after plugin construction. This is why admission plugins must not call the API server synchronously during `Admit`/`Validate` — they should read from their informer caches to avoid re-entrant requests.

Risk: A misbehaving admission webhook or plugin that times out degrades every write to the affected resource. The `FailurePolicy` field on `ValidatingWebhookConfiguration` controls whether a timeout causes a hard failure (`Fail`) or silently passes (`Ignore`). Choosing `Ignore` for security-sensitive policies is dangerous; choosing `Fail` creates an availability dependency on the webhook endpoint.

### 5.4 Custom Handler Chain (`BuildHandlerChainFunc`)

`Config.BuildHandlerChainFunc` (line 186 in `pkg/server/config.go`) is a replaceable function. Replacing it entirely is almost never correct. The safe pattern is:

```go
config.BuildHandlerChainFunc = func(handler http.Handler, c *server.Config) http.Handler {
    handler = myCustomMiddleware(handler)
    return server.DefaultBuildHandlerChain(handler, c)
}
```

Inserting middleware _before_ calling `DefaultBuildHandlerChain` places it after all standard filters (inside the chain, close to the handler). Wrapping the result of `DefaultBuildHandlerChain` places it before all filters (outside the chain, first to see raw requests).

Risk: Custom middleware placed outside authentication can observe unauthenticated requests, including attacker-controlled data. Custom middleware placed inside authentication has access to `user.Info` from the context but runs after the request has already been admitted by the concurrency limiter.

### 5.5 Post-Start and Pre-Shutdown Hooks

`GenericAPIServer.AddPostStartHook(name, fn)` registers a `PostStartHookFunc` (`pkg/server/hooks.go:43`). Each hook runs in its own goroutine after the TLS listener is open. Hooks receive a `PostStartHookContext` containing a `LoopbackClientConfig` (privileged, certificate-authenticated connection to the local API server). This is the canonical way to run initialization that requires the server to be accepting requests (e.g., installing default `FlowSchema` objects, setting up CRD indices).

`AddPreShutdownHook` registers a `PreShutdownHookFunc` that runs before the shutdown delay period begins, allowing in-process controllers to quiesce before the server stops accepting traffic.

Risk: A post-start hook that never returns will block `readyz` from succeeding, making the server appear not-ready indefinitely. The `LivezGracePeriod` (`Config` field) provides a window during which `/livez` returns true even if post-start hooks have not completed, preventing premature process restart.

### 5.6 Custom Storage Backends

Replacing the etcd backend requires implementing `storage.Interface` (`pkg/storage/interfaces.go:169`): `Create`, `Delete`, `Watch`, `Get`, `GetList`, and `GuaranteedUpdate`. The `Versioner` sub-interface must produce monotonically increasing string `resourceVersion` values. The cacher wraps any `storage.Interface`, so a custom backend automatically gains the in-process watch cache as long as its `Watch` returns a valid `watch.Interface`.

Risk: `GuaranteedUpdate` semantics are subtle — it must implement compare-and-swap retry with the exact resource version. An incorrect implementation leads to lost updates or stale writes, which will corrupt controller reconciliation loops.

### 5.7 CRDs as the Primary Extension Mechanism

For most users, `CustomResourceDefinition` objects (managed by `staging/src/k8s.io/apiextensions-apiserver`) are the correct extension point. A CRD creates a dynamically-registered `storage.Interface` backed by etcd, complete with schema validation (via CEL or OpenAPI structural schemas), admission webhook support, conversion webhooks for multiple versions, and status/scale subresources.

CRDs avoid the operational burden of deploying and securing a separate API server process. Their main limitation is that they use the unstructured `map[string]interface{}` representation internally (no Go type safety at the server level) and cannot implement custom subresource semantics (like `/exec` or `/logs`) that require streaming protocols.

---

## 6. Failure Modes, Limits, and Design Tradeoffs

### 6.1 etcd as the Single Source of Truth

All persistent state lives in etcd. The apiserver is stateless by design — any instance can serve any request. This enables horizontal scaling and zero-downtime rolling upgrades, but it means etcd is the availability bottleneck. An etcd leader election stall or a network partition between the apiserver and etcd will cause all write operations and consistent reads to fail with 503 or timeout.

The `storage.Interface.GuaranteedUpdate` loop retries on `resourceVersion` conflict but does not retry on etcd connectivity errors; those are surfaced as errors to the caller.

### 6.2 Optimistic Concurrency and 409 Conflicts

Updates carry the client's last-seen `resourceVersion`. The etcd3 store issues a transaction (`txn`) that only commits if the current `mod_revision` matches. A mismatch returns a `409 Conflict`. The `OptimisticLockErrorMsg` constant (`pkg/registry/generic/registry/store.go:262`) is the message returned to the client. Controllers should handle 409 by re-fetching the object from their informer cache and retrying, not by incrementing a counter and giving up.

High-contention resources (e.g., frequently-reconciled status fields) generate high 409 rates. Server-side apply (`PATCH` with `application/apply-patch+yaml`) mitigates this for field-manager-aware clients by merging at the field level rather than the object level.

### 6.3 Watch Cache Lag

The cacher introduces a small propagation delay between an etcd write and the event becoming visible to watch subscribers. Under normal conditions this is sub-second. Under high load, if the cacher's event processing goroutine falls behind, clients may see stale watch events. If a client's watch falls outside the `EventFreshDuration` window (default ~75 seconds), it receives a `410 Gone` and must perform a full relist. Frequent relisters under load create a feedback loop that further increases etcd read traffic.

### 6.4 API Priority and Fairness vs. the Simple Semaphore

When `FlowControl` is non-nil, `WithPriorityAndFairness` replaces the simpler `WithMaxInFlightLimit` semaphore. APF provides fine-grained control but introduces its own failure modes: misconfigured `FlowSchema` objects can accidentally starve critical system controllers, and the APF controller itself requires API server access to read its configuration — a bootstrap dependency handled by compiling in default `FlowSchema` objects.

The seat estimator for list requests (`flowcontrolrequest.NewWorkEstimator`) uses `StorageObjectCountTracker` to estimate how many objects a list will return, assigning proportionally more seats to large lists. This prevents a single unbounded `LIST` from consuming all concurrency slots.

### 6.5 Request Body Size and Timeout Limits

`Config.MaxRequestBodyBytes` (default 3 MiB for most resources) limits the deserialized request size. `Config.RequestTimeout` (default 60 seconds) is enforced by `WithTimeoutForNonLongRunningRequests` — the handler goroutine is cancelled after this duration. Long-running requests (watches, exec, port-forward) are exempted via `LongRunningFunc`.

The `GoawayChance` field (default 0, max 0.02) causes the server to send HTTP/2 GOAWAY frames with a configurable probability, nudging clients to reconnect and rebalance across server replicas. At high values this can disrupt long-running watches.

### 6.6 The Hub-and-Spoke Conversion Model

The `runtime.Scheme` uses a hub version (the internal Go type) for all cross-version conversions: to convert from `apps/v1beta1` to `apps/v1`, the scheme first converts to the internal hub version and then to the target. This means N versions require only 2N conversion functions rather than N². The tradeoff is that all versions must be representable in the internal hub type. Fields present only in a newer version must be stored in the internal type even when the cluster's storage version is older, creating forward-compatibility complexity.

---

## 7. Operational Implications

### 7.1 Graceful Shutdown Sequencing

Graceful termination is coordinated through the `lifecycleSignals` struct (`pkg/server/lifecycle_signals.go`), which exposes named channels for each phase:

1. **`ShutdownInitiated`** (on SIGTERM): `/readyz` begins returning failure. Pre-shutdown hooks run in parallel.
2. **`AfterShutdownDelayDuration`** (default +70s): The TLS listener stops accepting connections. If `ShutdownSendRetryAfter` is true, the server continues listening but replies to new requests with 429 + `Retry-After: 1`.
3. **`InFlightRequestsDrained`** (up to `ShutdownTimeout` = 60s): Ongoing non-long-running requests are given up to 60 seconds to complete.
4. Long-running requests (watches) are given a separate `ShutdownWatchTerminationGracePeriod` window.
5. Audit backend is flushed and storage is destroyed.

Load balancers upstream of the apiserver must be configured with a health-check interval shorter than 70 seconds so that traffic is redirected before the listener stops. The `ShutdownDelayDuration` provides the window for endpoints to converge on all nodes.

### 7.2 Health Endpoints

Three health endpoints serve different audiences:

- `/healthz` — liveness probe. Returns 200 as long as the process is not terminally broken. Continues returning 200 through the `ShutdownDelayDuration` period so load balancers can drain gracefully.
- `/readyz` — readiness probe. Returns failure during startup (until post-start hooks complete and storage is ready, managed by `StorageReadinessHook`) and during shutdown (as soon as `ShutdownInitiated`). Individual checks can be deregistered with `InstallReadyzCheck`.
- `/livez` — a newer liveness endpoint that distinguishes between transient and permanent failures. It applies `LivezGracePeriod` at startup.

Storage readiness checks are registered automatically for every resource whose `rest.Storage` implementation also implements `rest.StorageWithReadiness` (`pkg/registry/rest/rest.go:70`). A resource whose backing etcd prefix is inaccessible will cause `/readyz` to fail.

### 7.3 Observability

**Metrics** are exposed at `/metrics` via Prometheus. Key metrics include:
- `apiserver_request_total` — counter by verb, resource, group, version, code.
- `apiserver_request_duration_seconds` — histogram by verb, resource.
- `apiserver_current_inflight_requests` — gauge by request type (mutating/readonly).
- `etcd_request_duration_seconds` — etcd operation latency.
- `apiserver_watch_events_total` — watch event fan-out rate.
- APF metrics under `apiserver_flowcontrol_*`.

**Distributed tracing** (OpenTelemetry) is enabled when `APIServerTracing` feature gate is on. Traces start after authentication so the span includes the user identity. The `TracerProvider` is injected into the `Config` (`pkg/server/config.go:179`) and used in `filterlatency.TrackStarted`/`TrackCompleted` wrappers around each filter in the handler chain.

### 7.4 Performance Characteristics of List vs. Watch

A `LIST` without a `resourceVersion` (or with `rv=""`) triggers a consistent read: the server fetches the latest etcd revision, waits for the cache to catch up, and serves from the in-process store. This is cheap compared to a direct etcd range query.

A `LIST` with `resourceVersion=0` is served directly from the cache snapshot without an etcd round-trip. This is the most performant option but may return slightly stale data.

A cold `LIST` (cache miss, or a list of a resource type with no cacher) falls through to a direct paginated etcd range scan. On large clusters with hundreds of thousands of objects, these can saturate etcd I/O. Operators should ensure controllers use informers with `resourceVersion=0` initial lists and then transition to watch — not repeated list-polls.

### 7.5 Maintainability: The Strategy / Storage Separation

The `genericregistry.Store` architecture deliberately separates generic CRUD machinery from resource-specific semantics. Adding a new resource requires implementing only the `Strategy` interfaces; the HTTP handler, serialization, versioning, admission dispatch, etcd integration, and watch cache are inherited for free. This separation is the single most important maintainability property of the library: the 180+ resource types in the core Kubernetes API share identical CRUD infrastructure while each having fully custom validation and defaulting logic.

The cost is indirection — understanding what happens on a `PUT /apis/apps/v1/.../deployments/foo` requires tracing through the handler chain, the `APIInstaller`, the `Store.Update` method, the `DryRunnableStorage` wrapper, the `Cacher`, and finally the `etcd3.store`. Each layer is individually testable, but the full path is long enough that integration tests are essential.

---

## 8. File and Directory Map

The following lists the most important files and directories consulted in this analysis, with a brief statement of what each contains.

```
staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/
  ARCHITECTURE.md                         -- Official architectural overview (server chain, handler chain, API registration, watch cache, conflict resolution, discovery, security, streaming)
  pkg/server/
    config.go                             -- Config and RecommendedConfig structs; DefaultBuildHandlerChain (line 1015) assembles the HTTP middleware stack in reverse order
    genericapiserver.go                   -- GenericAPIServer struct; APIGroupInfo struct (line 71); InstallAPIGroups (line 876); DelegationTarget interface (line 308); PrepareRun / RunWithContext
    hooks.go                              -- PostStartHookFunc and PreShutdownHookFunc types; PostStartHookContext
    lifecycle_signals.go                  -- Named channel signals coordinating the graceful shutdown state machine
    secure_serving.go                     -- TLS listener setup and HTTP/2 configuration
    handler.go                            -- APIServerHandler: wraps go-restful Container and NonGoRestfulMux
    options/
      recommended.go                      -- RecommendedOptions: the standard set of options for an extension API server
      etcd.go                             -- EtcdOptions, storage backend configuration
      admission.go                        -- AdmissionOptions, plugin registration and initialization
  pkg/endpoints/
    installer.go                          -- APIInstaller: maps rest.Storage capability interfaces to HTTP verb registrations
    handlers/
      create.go                           -- createHandler: deserialization, admission, field manager, storage write for POST
      get.go                              -- getHandler: cache-aware GET
      update.go                           -- updateHandler: PATCH and PUT, optimistic concurrency
      delete.go                           -- deleteHandler: graceful deletion, finalizer logic
      watch.go                            -- watchHandler: watch channel setup and event streaming
    filters/
      authentication.go                   -- WithAuthentication filter
      authorization.go                    -- WithAuthorization filter
    request/
      context.go                          -- Request context accessors (WithUser, UserFrom, WithNamespace, etc.)
  pkg/admission/
    interfaces.go                         -- Attributes, MutationInterface, ValidationInterface, ObjectInterfaces
    chain.go                              -- chainAdmissionHandler: two-pass (Admit, Validate) iteration
    handler.go                            -- admission.Handler: base struct for plugins (Handles, SetReadyFunc)
    plugins.go                            -- Plugin registry and factory registration
    initializer/
      initializer.go                      -- PluginInitializer: injects informers, clients into plugins via interface detection
    plugin/policy/
      generic/plugin.go                   -- AdmissionPolicyManager[H]: generic CEL-based policy plugin (VAP, MAP)
      validating/dispatcher.go            -- ValidatingAdmissionPolicy dispatcher
      mutating/dispatcher.go              -- MutatingAdmissionPolicy dispatcher
  pkg/registry/
    rest/rest.go                          -- Core rest.Storage capability interfaces (Getter, Creater, Updater, Deleter, Watcher, Lister, etc.)
    generic/registry/store.go             -- genericregistry.Store: canonical CRUD storage with Strategy hooks
  pkg/storage/
    interfaces.go                         -- storage.Interface (the abstract storage contract), Versioner, Preconditions, UpdateFunc
    etcd3/store.go                        -- etcd3 implementation; GuaranteedUpdate CAS retry loop; encryption integration
    cacher/cacher.go                      -- Cacher: in-process watch cache wrapping storage.Interface; Config struct; bookmark handling
    value/
      transformer.go                      -- value.Transformer interface for at-rest encryption
      encrypt/envelope/envelope.go        -- KMS v1 envelope encryption via gRPC
      encrypt/envelope/kmsv2/            -- KMS v2 implementation (AES-GCM, DEK caching)
  pkg/authentication/
    request/union/union.go               -- Union authenticator: first-success chain
  pkg/authorization/
    authorizer/interfaces.go              -- authorizer.Authorizer, Decision (Allow/Deny/NoOpinion)
  pkg/util/flowcontrol/
    apf_filter.go                         -- WithPriorityAndFairness HTTP filter
    apf_controller.go                     -- APF controller: watches FlowSchema/PriorityLevel, configures queues
    request/work_estimator.go             -- Seat-count estimator for list requests (uses StorageObjectCountTracker)
  pkg/audit/
    policy/checker.go                     -- PolicyRuleEvaluator: matches requests to audit rules and levels

cmd/kube-apiserver/
  app/server.go                           -- CreateServerChain (line 176): constructs the three-server delegation chain; entry point for the kube-apiserver binary

staging/src/k8s.io/kube-aggregator/
  pkg/apiserver/                          -- Aggregation layer: watches APIService objects, proxies requests to extension API servers

staging/src/k8s.io/apiextensions-apiserver/
  pkg/apiserver/                          -- CRD server: dynamically installs/removes REST handlers when CRDs are created/deleted

staging/src/k8s.io/sample-apiserver/
  pkg/                                    -- Reference implementation of an extension API server using RecommendedOptions
```

---

*Analysis based on the kubernetes/kubernetes repository at commit `8c9c67c0`, March 2026.*

# Task: Kubernetes API Server Architecture and Extension Guide

**Repository:** kubernetes/kubernetes (stripped snapshot)
**Output:** Write your document to `/workspace/documentation.md`

## Objective

Produce a subsystem architecture and extension guide for the apiserver library with request-path flow and extension tradeoffs.

## Scope

Focus on the Kubernetes apiserver library subsystem.
Your document must explain component responsibilities, end-to-end flow, and extension or operational tradeoffs.

## Content Expectations

Address all of the following in your own structure:
- subsystem purpose, boundaries, and upstream/downstream dependencies
- key components and how responsibilities are split
- end-to-end control/request flow with concrete repository evidence
- failure modes, limits, and design tradeoffs
- extension points, customization hooks, and associated risks
- a concise map of the most important files/directories used in your analysis

## Quality Bar

- Cite concrete file paths from the repository.
- Explain behavior and interactions, not just API signatures.
- Include at least one section on operational implications (performance, reliability, or maintainability).
- Do not fabricate classes, functions, or files.

## Anti-Requirements

- Do not output `doc.go`.
- Do not provide shallow bullet dumps without system flow explanation.
- Do not rely on external docs not present in the workspace.
Now I have enough information to write a thorough architecture guide. Let me compose it.

The document has been written to `/workspace/documentation.md`. Here is a summary of what it covers and the concrete evidence it draws from:

---

**What the document covers:**

1. **Subsystem Purpose and Boundaries** — Defines the library's role as a shared framework, its upstream dependencies (`apimachinery`, `client-go`, etcd client, `kube-openapi`), and downstream consumers (the three servers in `kube-apiserver`, extension API servers).

2. **Key Components** — Eight subsystems with responsibility splits:
   - `Config` / `RecommendedOptions` / `GenericAPIServer` (bootstrap)
   - The three-server delegation chain (`CreateServerChain` at `cmd/kube-apiserver/app/server.go:176`)
   - The HTTP handler chain (`DefaultBuildHandlerChain` at `pkg/server/config.go:1015`), explained in the correct inside-out traversal order with every filter named
   - `APIGroupInfo` + `APIInstaller` (`pkg/endpoints/installer.go`), the capability-interface routing mechanism
   - `genericregistry.Store` (`pkg/registry/generic/registry/store.go`) and the Strategy pattern
   - `storage.Interface` + `etcd3.store` + `Cacher` (storage layer)
   - The two-pass (`Admit`/`Validate`) admission chain (`pkg/admission/chain.go`)
   - Audit policy (`pkg/audit/policy/checker.go`)

3. **End-to-End Request Flow** — Traces a server-side apply PATCH through TLS ingress, authentication, APF queuing, authorization, routing, deserialization, mutation admission, validation admission, etcd CAS write, and watch cache propagation — with file and line references at each step.

4. **Watch Cache and Storage Layer** — Initialization, consistent-read semantics, bookmark events, and the KMS v2 encryption-at-rest chain.

5. **Extension Points** — Six concrete hooks: `RecommendedOptions` extension API server pattern, custom Strategy objects, custom admission plugins (with the `PluginInitializer` dependency injection mechanism), `BuildHandlerChainFunc` replacement, post-start/pre-shutdown hooks, and custom `storage.Interface` backends. Each includes an associated risk.

6. **Failure Modes and Tradeoffs** — etcd as availability bottleneck, 409 conflict storms, watch cache lag and relist feedback loops, APF misconfiguration risks, request size/timeout limits, GOAWAY, and hub-and-spoke conversion complexity.

7. **Operational Implications** — Graceful shutdown sequencing (the 70s + 60s two-phase sequence), three health endpoints and what each signals, Prometheus metrics, distributed tracing integration, and the List vs. Watch performance characteristics.

8. **File/Directory Map** — 30+ concrete file paths with one-line descriptions of what each contains.

1→# apiserver Architecture
     2→
     3→## 1. Server Composition
     4→
     5→The `kube-apiserver` binary is not one server, but a **server chain** of three distinct
     6→`GenericAPIServer` instances: the core Kubernetes API, API extensions (CRDs), and the aggregation
     7→layer.
     8→
     9→This composition is managed by a layered configuration system, starting with command-line
    10→flags parsed by `options` structs (e.g., `RecommendedOptions` in `pkg/server/options`), which
    11→populate a `Config` object that is then used to instantiate the `GenericAPIServer`
    12→instances. The construction of this delegation chain can be found in the `CreateServerChain`
    13→function in `cmd/kube-apiserver/app/server.go`.
    14→
    15→```mermaid
    16→graph TD
    17→    subgraph Incoming Request
    18→        direction LR
    19→        A[User/Client] --> B{/apis/apps/v1/deployments};
    20→    end
    21→
    22→    subgraph kube-apiserver process
    23→        direction LR
    24→        B --> C[Aggregator Server];
    25→        C -- Not an APIService --> D[Kube API Server];
    26→        D -- Handles Request --> E[REST Storage];
    27→        C -- Is an APIService --> F[Proxy to Extension API Server];
    28→        D -- Not a Core API --> G[API Extensions Server];
    29→        G -- Handles CRD --> E;
    30→    end
    31→```
    32→
    33→1.  **Aggregator Server (`kube-aggregator`):**
    34→    *   **Purpose:** Handles the `apiregistration.k8s.io` API and acts as a reverse proxy for
    35→        extension API servers. This functionality was designed to allow third-party APIs to be
    36→        "aggregated" into the main Kubernetes API server seamlessly.
    37→    *   **Mechanism:** It watches `APIService` objects. When a request arrives (e.g., for
    38→        `/apis/mycompany.com/v1/myresources`), it checks if an `APIService` has "claimed"
    39→        that path. If so, it uses a `ServiceResolver` to find the IP of the backing
    40→        `Service` and proxies the request.
    41→    *   **Use Case:** This pattern is for programmatic, high-control extensions that require
    42→        custom business logic (e.g., non-CRUD subresources like `/logs`) or alternative
    43→        storage backends.
    44→    *   **Delegation:** If no `APIService` matches, it delegates the request to the next
    45→        server in the chain.
    46→
    47→2.  **Kube API Server (Core):**
    48→    *   **Purpose:** Serves all the built-in Kubernetes APIs (`core/v1`, `apps/v1`, etc.).
    49→    *   **Mechanism:** This is the main server, configured with all the core REST storage
    50→        strategies.
    51→    *   **Delegation:** If a request is for a path that is not a core API (e.g., for a CRD),
    52→        it delegates the request to the next server in the chain.
    53→
    54→3.  **API Extensions Server (`apiextensions-apiserver`):**
    55→    *   **Purpose:** Handles the `apiextensions.k8s.io` API, which manages
    56→        `CustomResourceDefinition` objects (CRDs). The evolution of CRDs from a simple extension
    57→        mechanism to a feature-rich system with validation, versioning, and defaulting is
    58→        documented in a series of KEPs, starting with the graduation to GA in Kubernetes v1.16.
    59→    *   **Mechanism:** When a CRD is created, this server dynamically creates and installs a
    60→        new REST storage handler for the new resource, making it immediately available.
    61→    *   **Use Case:** CRDs are the most common extension pattern, offering a declarative,
    62→        schema-based way to define new resource types that are stored in etcd and require no
    63→        custom API server code.
    64→    *   **Delegation:** It is the end of the chain. If it cannot handle a request, a `404 Not
    65→        Found` is returned.
    66→
    67→## 2. Handler Chain
    68→
    69→Every request flows through a standard chain of HTTP handlers (filters). The request body
    70→is not deserialized until it has passed authentication and authorization. The default handler
    71→chain is constructed by the `DefaultBuildHandlerChain` function in
    72→`staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/config.go`.
    73→
    74→```mermaid
    75→sequenceDiagram
    76→    participant Client
    77→    participant Handler Chain
    78→    participant Authentication
    79→    participant Authorization
    80→    participant Priority and Fairness
    81→    participant Admission Control
    82→    participant REST Endpoint
    83→
    84→    Client->>Handler Chain: Request
    85→    Handler Chain->>Authentication: Authenticate
    86→    Authentication-->>Handler Chain: User Info
    87→    Handler Chain->>Authorization: Authorize
    88→    Authorization-->>Handler Chain: Allowed/Denied
    89→    Handler Chain->>Priority and Fairness: Classify & Queue
    90→    Priority and Fairness-->>Handler Chain: Proceed
    91→    Handler Chain->>Admission Control: Mutate & Validate
    92→    Admission Control-->>Handler Chain: Object OK
    93→    Handler Chain->>REST Endpoint: Handle
    94→    REST Endpoint-->>Handler Chain: Response
    95→    Handler Chain-->>Client: Response
    96→```
    97→
    98→The handler chain consists of the following stages:
    99→
   100→1.  **Authentication (`pkg/authentication`):** This filter identifies the user. The system is
   101→    pluggable and composed of multiple authenticators (e.g., client certs, bearer tokens, OIDC).
   102→    The identity of the user is determined by the first authenticator in the chain that successfully
   103→    identifies the user.
   104→2.  **Authorization (`pkg/authorization`):** This filter checks if the user is permitted to
   105→    perform the action. This system is also pluggable and composed of multiple authorizers
   106→    (e.g., RBAC, Node, Webhook). Each authorizer may respond with either allow, deny, or no opinion.
   107→    If the response is no opinion, the request is passed to the next authorizer in the chain.
   108→3.  **Priority and Fairness (`pkg/util/flowcontrol`):** This subsystem manages request
   109→    concurrency, classifying requests into `FlowSchema`s and `PriorityLevel`s to prevent
   110→    overload. This feature was introduced to prevent high traffic from overwhelming the API
   111→    server and to ensure that critical cluster operations are not starved.
   112→4.  **Admission Control (`pkg/admission`):** This is the primary mechanism for policy
   113→    enforcement. It is only at this stage that the request body is deserialized into an
   114→    object. It is a chain of plugins that can mutate or validate an object. The built-in Pod
   115→    Security admission controller is a key example of this, enforcing Pod Security Standards
   116→    at the namespace level.
   117→5.  **REST Endpoint Handling (`pkg/endpoints`):** The request is finally dispatched to the
   118→    appropriate REST handler, which is installed by the `APIInstaller`.
   119→
   120→## 3. API Group Registration
   121→
   122→The high-level steps for introducing an API are:
   123→
   124→1.  **Define Types:** Create or modify the Go structs in the `types.go` file for the API group.
   125→2.  **Generate Code:** Use the code generators provided by the Kubernetes project to create the required
   126→    boilerplate methods for deep-copy, conversion, and defaulting.
   127→3.  **Implement the `Strategy`:** Write the custom business logic and validation for the
   128→    resource in its `Strategy` object.
   129→4.  **Register and Install:** Create the `APIGroupInfo` struct, bundling the `Scheme` and the
   130→    `Strategy`-configured storage, and pass it to the `GenericAPIServer`'s `InstallAPIGroup`
   131→    method.
   132→
   133→### The API Group Registry
   134→
   135→The `runtime.Scheme` acts as a central registry for an API group's type information. A single
   136→`Scheme` object is created for each API group and is responsible for the following key
   137→capabilities:
   138→
   139→*   **Type Registration and Mapping:** The `Scheme`'s primary role is to map a GroupVersionKind
   140→    (GVK) to its corresponding Go type and back. This process also relies on the `deepcopy-gen`
   141→    tool to create `DeepCopy()` methods for each type, which is critical for ensuring that
   142→    objects returned from caches are never modified directly.
   143→
   144→*   **API Conversion:** The `Scheme` stores the conversion functions that translate objects
   145→    between different API versions. These functions are typically generated by the
   146→    `conversion-gen` tool and enable the **hub-and-spoke** model.
   147→
   148→*   **Defaulting:** The `Scheme` registers defaulting functions that populate optional fields in
   149→    an object. These are usually generated by the `defaulter-gen` tool.
   150→
   151→*   **Declarative Validation:** The `Scheme` can store and execute code-generated validation
   152→    functions, providing a baseline level of validation. This is distinct from the primary,
   153→    handwritten business logic validation, which is handled by the `Strategy` object.
   154→
   155→### The `APIGroupInfo` Struct and `Strategy` Object
   156→
   157→With a populated `Scheme`, the API group is registered with the `GenericAPIServer` by bundling
   158→the `Scheme` with the storage backend and versioning information into an `APIGroupInfo` struct.
   159→
   160→```mermaid
   161→graph TD
   162→    subgraph Server Configuration
   163→        A[APIGroupInfo for apps v1];
   164→        A --> B{Scheme: Knows Deployment v1};
   165→        A --> C{Storage: deployments RESTStorage};
   166→        A --> D{Version Priority: v1, v1beta1};
   167→    end
   168→
   169→    subgraph RESTStorage Implementation
   170→        C --> E[genericregistry.Store];
   171→        E --> F[etcd client];
   172→        E --> G[Deployment Strategy];
   173→    end
   174→
   175→    subgraph Server Runtime
   176→        H[GenericAPIServer] -- InstallAPIGroup --> I[APIInstaller];
   177→        I -- Uses --> A;
   178→        I --> J{Register /apis/apps/v1/deployments};
   179→        J --> K[HTTP Handler];
   180→        K -- On Request --> C;
   181→    end
   182→```
   183→
   184→The registration process follows these steps:
   185→
   186→1.  **`APIGroupInfo` Construction:** For each API group, an `APIGroupInfo` struct is created,
   187→    which contains the populated `Scheme`, a map of resources to their storage
   188→    implementations, and an ordered list of **Version Priority**.
   189→
   190→2.  **REST Storage Instantiation:** For each resource, a `genericregistry.Store` is created. It
   191→    is configured with a resource-specific `Strategy` object that contains the core business
   192→    logic (e.g., handwritten validation).
   193→
   194→3.  **API Group Installation:** The `GenericAPIServer`'s `InstallAPIGroup` method takes the
   195→    `APIGroupInfo` and uses an `APIInstaller` to expose the resources as HTTP endpoints.
   196→
   197→## 4. Watch Cache
   198→
   199→To handle the high volume of watch requests from controllers without overwhelming etcd, the
   200→apiserver uses a **watch cache**. The implementation can be found in
   201→`staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/`.
   202→
   203→*   **Initialization:** The cacher first performs a `LIST` to get the current state of all
   204→    objects and a `ResourceVersion` for that point-in-time. It then starts a `WATCH` from
   205→    that version to ensure a consistent stream of events.
   206→*   **Serving from Cache:** Most list and watch requests are served from this in-memory cache, which
   207→    dramatically reduces the load on etcd. Consistent reads are also served from the
   208→    cache. This is achieved by first fetching the revision number of the latest write from
   209→    etcd. The server then ensures the cache is at least that recent—waiting for it to
   210→    refresh if necessary—before serving the request.
   211→*   **Fallback to Storage:** If a client request cannot be served from the
   212→    cache's buffer, the request "falls through" to the underlying etcd storage.
   213→*   **Bookmarks:** The cacher uses bookmark events to track the latest `ResourceVersion` for
   214→    unchanged objects. This prevents the cache's `ResourceVersion` from becoming too old,
   215→    which avoids the need for expensive relist operations from etcd when the objects have
   216→    not been modified.
   217→
   218→## 5. Conflict Resolution
   219→
   220→*   **Optimistic Concurrency via `resourceVersion`:** Clients are expected to perform updates using a
   221→    read-modify-write workflow. The apiserver uses the `resourceVersion` field of every
   222→    object to enforce optimistic concurrency. This `resourceVersion` is not an arbitrary number;
   223→    it maps directly to etcd's globally consistent `mod_revision`. When a client submits an
   224→    update (`PUT` or `PATCH`), it must provide the `resourceVersion` of the object it based its
   225→    modifications on. If the `resourceVersion` on the server does not match the current
   226→    `mod_revision` in etcd, the server rejects the request with a `409 Conflict` error. This
   227→    forces the client to re-read the object, resolve the conflict, and resubmit with the new
   228→    `resourceVersion`.
   229→*   **Server-Side Apply:** A declarative, "intent-based" patch. The server maintains a
   230→    `managedFields` section in the object's metadata to track which "manager" (e.g., a
   231→    controller) owns each field. This allows multiple actors to manage different parts of the
   232→    same object without overwriting each other's changes.
   233→
   234→## 6. Discovery and OpenAPI
   235→
   236→Apiservers serve the `/apis` discovery endpoints and the `/openapi/v2` and `/openapi/v3`
   237→specifications. The generation of the OpenAPI specification is a multi-stage process.
   238→
   239→*   **`openapi-gen`**: This tool reflects on Go structs, reads godoc comments, and looks at
   240→    validation struct tags to generate a map of all API definitions.
   241→*   **`zz_generated.openapi.go`**: The output is a large Go file containing a
   242→    `GetOpenAPIDefinitions` function.
   243→*   **Runtime**: The `GenericAPIServer` calls this generated function to build the final OpenAPI
   244→    JSON spec that it serves to clients.
   245→
   246→## 7. Security & Observability
   247→
   248→*   **Audit (`pkg/audit`):** The apiserver has a policy-driven event logging pipeline. The audit
   249→    policy controls what is logged and at which stage of a request.
   250→*   **Security:**
   251→    *   **mTLS:** The primary authentication mechanism for system components.
   252→    *   **Service Account Token Issuance:** The `kube-apiserver` acts as an OIDC provider,
   253→        issuing and validating JWTs for `ServiceAccount`s.
   254→
   255→## 8. Streaming Protocols
   256→
   257→*   **Websockets:** The apiserver uses websockets to upgrade HTTP
   258→    connections for interactive, streaming protocols like `exec`, `attach`, and
   259→    `port-forward`. The `UpgradeAwareProxyHandler` manages this process.
   260→

This functionality was designed to allow third-party APIs to be\n        \"aggregated\" into the main Kubernetes API server seamlessly.\n    *   **Mechanism:** It watches `APIService` objects. When a request arrives (e.g., for\n        `/apis/mycompany.com/v1/myresources`), it checks if an `APIService` has \"claimed\"\n        that path. If so, it uses a `ServiceResolver` to find the IP of the backing\n        `Service` and proxies the request.\n    *   **Use Case:** This pattern is for programmatic, high-control extensions that require\n        custom business logic (e.g., non-CRUD subresources like `/logs`) or alternative\n        storage backends.\n    *   **Delegation:** If no `APIService` matches, it delegates the request to the next\n        server in the chain.\n\n2.  **Kube API Server (Core):**\n    *   **Purpose:** Serves all the built-in Kubernetes APIs (`core/v1`, `apps/v1`, etc.).\n    *   **Mechanism:** This is the main server, configured with all the core REST storage\n        strategies.\n    *   **Delegation:** If a request is for a path that is not a core API (e.g., for a CRD),\n        it delegates the request to the next server in the chain.\n\n3.  **API Extensions Server (`apiextensions-apiserver`):**\n    *   **Purpose:** Handles the `apiextensions.k8s.io` API, which manages\n        `CustomResourceDefinition` objects (CRDs). The evolution of CRDs from a simple extension\n        mechanism to a feature-rich system with validation, versioning, and defaulting is\n        documented in a series of KEPs, starting with the graduation to GA in Kubernetes v1.16.\n    *   **Mechanism:** When a CRD is created, this server dynamically creates and installs a\n        new REST storage handler for the new resource, making it immediately available.\n    *   **Use Case:** CRDs are the most common extension pattern, offering a declarative,\n        schema-based way to define new resource types that are stored in etcd and require no\n        custom API server code.\n    *   **Delegation:** It is the end of the chain. If it cannot handle a request, a `404 Not\n        Found` is returned.\n\n## 2. Handler Chain\n\nEvery request flows through a standard chain of HTTP handlers (filters). The request body\nis not deserialized until it has passed authentication and authorization. The default handler\nchain is constructed by the `DefaultBuildHandlerChain` function in\n`staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/server/config.go`.\n\n```mermaid\nsequenceDiagram\n    participant Client\n    participant Handler Chain\n    participant Authentication\n    participant Authorization\n    participant Priority and Fairness\n    participant Admission Control\n    participant REST Endpoint\n\n    Client->>Handler Chain: Request\n    Handler Chain->>Authentication: Authenticate\n    Authentication-->>Handler Chain: User Info\n    Handler Chain->>Authorization: Authorize\n    Authorization-->>Handler Chain: Allowed/Denied\n    Handler Chain->>Priority and Fairness: Classify & Queue\n    Priority and Fairness-->>Handler Chain: Proceed\n    Handler Chain->>Admission Control: Mutate & Validate\n    Admission Control-->>Handler Chain: Object OK\n    Handler Chain->>REST Endpoint: Handle\n    REST Endpoint-->>Handler Chain: Response\n    Handler Chain-->>Client: Response\n```\n\nThe handler chain consists of the following stages:\n\n1.  **Authentication (`pkg/authentication`):** This filter identifies the user. The system is\n    pluggable and composed of multiple authenticators (e.g., client certs, bearer tokens, OIDC).\n    The identity of the user is determined by the first authenticator in the chain that successfully\n    identifies the user.\n2.  **Authorization (`pkg/authorization`):** This filter checks if the user is permitted to\n    perform the action. This system is also pluggable and composed of multiple authorizers\n    (e.g., RBAC, Node, Webhook). Each authorizer may respond with either allow, deny, or no opinion.\n    If the response is no opinion, the request is passed to the next authorizer in the chain.\n3.  **Priority and Fairness (`pkg/util/flowcontrol`):** This subsystem manages request\n    concurrency, classifying requests into `FlowSchema`s and `PriorityLevel`s to prevent\n    overload. This feature was introduced to prevent high traffic from overwhelming the API\n    server and to ensure that critical cluster operations are not starved.\n4.  **Admission Control (`pkg/admission`):** This is the primary mechanism for policy\n    enforcement. It is only at this stage that the request body is deserialized into an\n    object. It is a chain of plugins that can mutate or validate an object. The built-in Pod\n    Security admission controller is a key example of this, enforcing Pod Security Standards\n    at the namespace level.\n5.  **REST Endpoint Handling (`pkg/endpoints`):** The request is finally dispatched to the\n    appropriate REST handler, which is installed by the `APIInstaller`.\n\n## 3. API Group Registration\n\nThe high-level steps for introducing an API are:\n\n1.  **Define Types:** Create or modify the Go structs in the `types.go` file for the API group.\n2.  **Generate Code:** Use the code generators provided by the Kubernetes project to create the required\n    boilerplate methods for deep-copy, conversion, and defaulting.\n3.  **Implement the `Strategy`:** Write the custom business logic and validation for the\n    resource in its `Strategy` object.\n4.  **Register and Install:** Create the `APIGroupInfo` struct, bundling the `Scheme` and the\n    `Strategy`-configured storage, and pass it to the `GenericAPIServer`'s `InstallAPIGroup`\n    method.\n\n### The API Group Registry\n\nThe `runtime.Scheme` acts as a central registry for an API group's type information. A single\n`Scheme` object is created for each API group and is responsible for the following key\ncapabilities:\n\n*   **Type Registration and Mapping:** The `Scheme`'s primary role is to map a GroupVersionKind\n    (GVK) to its corresponding Go type and back. This process also relies on the `deepcopy-gen`\n    tool to create `DeepCopy()` methods for each type, which is critical for ensuring that\n    objects returned from caches are never modified directly.\n\n*   **API Conversion:** The `Scheme` stores the conversion functions that translate objects\n    between different API versions. These functions are typically generated by the\n    `conversion-gen` tool and enable the **hub-and-spoke** model.\n\n*   **Defaulting:** The `Scheme` registers defaulting functions that populate optional fields in\n    an object. These are usually generated by the `defaulter-gen` tool.\n\n*   **Declarative Validation:** The `Scheme` can store and execute code-generated validation\n    functions, providing a baseline level of validation. This is distinct from the primary,\n    handwritten business logic validation, which is handled by the `Strategy` object.\n\n### The `APIGroupInfo` Struct and `Strategy` Object\n\nWith a populated `Scheme`, the API group is registered with the `GenericAPIServer` by bundling\nthe `Scheme` with the storage backend and versioning information into an `APIGroupInfo` struct.\n\n```mermaid\ngraph TD\n    subgraph Server Configuration\n        A[APIGroupInfo for apps v1];\n        A --> B{Scheme: Knows Deployment v1};\n        A --> C{Storage: deployments RESTStorage};\n        A --> D{Version Priority: v1, v1beta1};\n    end\n\n    subgraph RESTStorage Implementation\n        C --> E[genericregistry.Store];\n        E --> F[etcd client];\n        E --> G[Deployment Strategy];\n    end\n\n    subgraph Server Runtime\n        H[GenericAPIServer] -- InstallAPIGroup --> I[APIInstaller];\n        I -- Uses --> A;\n        I --> J{Register /apis/apps/v1/deployments};\n        J --> K[HTTP Handler];\n        K -- On Request --> C;\n    end\n```\n\nThe registration process follows these steps:\n\n1.  **`APIGroupInfo` Construction:** For each API group, an `APIGroupInfo` struct is created,\n    which contains the populated `Scheme`, a map of resources to their storage\n    implementations, and an ordered list of **Version Priority**.\n\n2.  **REST Storage Instantiation:** For each resource, a `genericregistry.Store` is created. It\n    is configured with a resource-specific `Strategy` object that contains the core business\n    logic (e.g., handwritten validation).\n\n3.  **API Group Installation:** The `GenericAPIServer`'s `InstallAPIGroup` method takes the\n    `APIGroupInfo` and uses an `APIInstaller` to expose the resources as HTTP endpoints.\n\n## 4. Watch Cache\n\nTo handle the high volume of watch requests from controllers without overwhelming etcd, the\napiserver uses a **watch cache**. The implementation can be found in\n`staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/`.\n\n*   **Initialization:** The cacher first performs a `LIST` to get the current state of all\n    objects and a `ResourceVersion` for that point-in-time. It then starts a `WATCH` from\n    that version to ensure a consistent stream of events.\n*   **Serving from Cache:** Most list and watch requests are served from this in-memory cache, which\n    dramatically reduces the load on etcd. Consistent reads are also served from the\n    cache. This is achieved by first fetching the revision number of the latest write from\n    etcd. The server then ensures the cache is at least that recent—waiting for it to\n    refresh if necessary—before serving the request.\n*   **Fallback to Storage:** If a client request cannot be served from the\n    cache's buffer, the request \"falls through\" to the underlying etcd storage.\n*   **Bookmarks:** The cacher uses bookmark events to track the latest `ResourceVersion` for\n    unchanged objects. This prevents the cache's `ResourceVersion` from becoming too old,\n    which avoids the need for expensive relist operations from etcd when the objects have\n    not been modified.\n\n## 5. Conflict Resolution\n\n*   **Optimistic Concurrency via `resourceVersion`:** Clients are expected to perform updates using a\n    read-modify-write workflow. The apiserver uses the `resourceVersion` field of every\n    object to enforce optimistic concurrency. This `resourceVersion` is not an arbitrary number;\n    it maps directly to etcd's globally consistent `mod_revision`. When a client submits an\n    update (`PUT` or `PATCH`), it must provide the `resourceVersion` of the object it based its\n    modifications on. If the `resourceVersion` on the server does not match the current\n    `mod_revision` in etcd, the server rejects the request with a `409 Conflict` error. This\n    forces the client to re-read the object, resolve the conflict, and resubmit with the new\n    `resourceVersion`.\n*   **Server-Side Apply:** A declarative, \"intent-based\" patch. The server maintains a\n    `managedFields` section in the object's metadata to track which \"manager\" (e.g., a\n    controller) owns each field. This allows multiple actors to manage different parts of the\n    same object without overwriting each other's changes.\n\n## 6. Discovery and OpenAPI\n\nApiservers serve the `/apis` discovery endpoints and the `/openapi/v2` and `/openapi/v3`\nspecifications. The generation of the OpenAPI specification is a multi-stage process.\n\n*   **`openapi-gen`**: This tool reflects on Go structs, reads godoc comments, and looks at\n    validation struct tags to generate a map of all API definitions.\n*   **`zz_generated.openapi.go`**: The output is a large Go file containing a\n    `GetOpenAPIDefinitions` function.\n*   **Runtime**: The `GenericAPIServer` calls this generated function to build the final OpenAPI\n    JSON spec that it serves to clients.\n\n## 7. Security & Observability\n\n*   **Audit (`pkg/audit`):** The apiserver has a policy-driven event logging pipeline. The audit\n    policy controls what is logged and at which stage of a request.\n*   **Security:**\n    *   **mTLS:** The primary authentication mechanism for system components.\n    *   **Service Account Token Issuance:** The `kube-apiserver` acts as an OIDC provider,\n        issuing and validating JWTs for `ServiceAccount`s.\n\n## 8. Streaming Protocols\n\n*   **Websockets:** The apiserver uses websockets to upgrade HTTP\n    connections for interactive, streaming protocols like `exec`, `attach`, and\n    `port-forward`. The `UpgradeAwareProxyHandler` manages this process.\n", "numLines": 260, "startLine": 1, "totalLines": 260}}

    70→// Info about an API group.
    71→type APIGroupInfo struct {
    72→	PrioritizedVersions []schema.GroupVersion
    73→	// Info about the resources in this group. It's a map from version to resource to the storage.
    74→	VersionedResourcesStorageMap map[string]map[string]rest.Storage
    75→	// OptionsExternalVersion controls the APIVersion used for common objects in the
    76→	// schema like api.Status, api.DeleteOptions, and metav1.ListOptions. Other implementors may
    77→	// define a version "v1beta1" but want to use the Kubernetes "v1" internal objects.
    78→	// If nil, defaults to groupMeta.GroupVersion.
    79→	// TODO: Remove this when https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/19018 is fixed.
    80→	OptionsExternalVersion *schema.GroupVersion
    81→	// MetaGroupVersion defaults to "meta.k8s.io/v1" and is the scheme group version used to decode
    82→	// common API implementations like ListOptions. Future changes will allow this to vary by group
    83→	// version (for when the inevitable meta/v2 group emerges).
    84→	MetaGroupVersion *schema.GroupVersion
    85→
    86→	// Scheme includes all of the types used by this group and how to convert between them (or
    87→	// to convert objects from outside of this group that are accepted in this API).
    88→	// TODO: replace with interfaces
    89→	Scheme *runtime.Scheme
    90→	// NegotiatedSerializer controls how this group encodes and decodes data
    91→	NegotiatedSerializer runtime.NegotiatedSerializer
    92→	// ParameterCodec performs conversions for query parameters passed to API calls
    93→	ParameterCodec runtime.ParameterCodec
    94→
    95→	// StaticOpenAPISpec is the spec derived from the definitions of all resources installed together.
    96→	// It is set during InstallAPIGroups, InstallAPIGroup, and InstallLegacyAPIGroup.
    97→	StaticOpenAPISpec map[string]*spec.Schema
    98→}

emulation version is set.\n\t// Not applicable to alpha APIs.\n\tEmulationForwardCompatible bool\n\t// RuntimeConfigEmulationForwardCompatible is an option to explicitly enable specific APIs introduced after the emulation version through the runtime-config.\n\t// If true, APIs identified by group/version that are enabled in the --runtime-config flag will be installed even if it is introduced after the emulation version. --runtime-config flag values that identify multiple APIs, such as api/all,api/ga,api/beta, are not influenced by this flag and will only enable APIs available at the current emulation version.\n\t// If false, error would be thrown if any GroupVersion or GroupVersionResource explicitly enabled in the --runtime-config flag is introduced after the emulation version.\n\tRuntimeConfigEmulationForwardCompatible bool\n\t// FeatureGate is a way to plumb feature gate through if you have them.\n\tFeatureGate featuregate.FeatureGate\n\t// AuditBackend is where audit events are sent to.\n\tAuditBackend audit.Backend\n\t// AuditPolicyRuleEvaluator makes the decision of whether and how to audit log a request.\n\tAuditPolicyRuleEvaluator audit.PolicyRuleEvaluator\n\t// ExternalAddress is the host name to use for external (public internet) facing URLs (e.g. Swagger)\n\t// Will default to a value based on secure serving info and non long-running requests\n\t// to complete while the server is shuting down.\n\tNonLongRunningRequestWaitGroup *utilwaitgroup.SafeWaitGroup\n\t// WatchRequestWaitGroup allows us to wait for all chain\n\t// handlers associated with active watch requests to\n\t// complete while the server is shuting down.\n\tWatchRequestWaitGroup *utilwaitgroup.RateLimitedSafeWaitGroup\n\t// DiscoveryAddresses is used to build the IPs pass to discovery. If nil, the ExternalAddress is\n\t// always reported\n\tDiscoveryAddresses discovery.Addresses\n\t// The default set of healthz checks. Note that it is up to the request handlers to ignore or honor this timeout. In seconds.\n\tMinRequestTimeout int\n\n\t// StorageInitializationTimeout defines the maximum amount of time to wait for storage initialization\n\t// before declaring apiserver ready.\n\tStorageInitializationTimeout time.Duration\n\n\t// This represents the maximum amount of time it should take for apiserver to complete its startup\n\t// sequence and become healthy. From apiserver's start time to when this amount of time has\n\t// elapsed, /livez will assume that unfinished post-start hooks will complete successfully and\n\t// therefore return true.\n\tLivezGracePeriod time.Duration\n\t// ShutdownDelayDuration allows to block shutdown for some time, e.g. until endpoints pointing to this API server\n\t// have converged on all node.
